title: Slow-cooker pea and ham soup
subtitle: Split peas and a ham hock, cooked to a thick, smoky soup.
excerpt: A ham hock and yellow split peas slow-cooked together for eight hours until the peas dissolve into a thick, smoky soup. The ham falls off the bone and shreds back into the pot. Simple, cheap, and deeply warming. Serves six.
sourceNotes: Pea and ham soup. Split pea soups appear in Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), 'Pea Soup'. The ham-hock version is a long-established British working-class thrift dish; early 20th-century references appear in the School of Domestic Economy collections.

content.text: Everything goes in raw. A ham hock, a mugful of yellow split peas, sliced onion and carrot, bay leaves, enough water to cover. Eight hours on LOW, and the peas dissolve into the liquid while the ham falls off the bone. Shred the meat back in and that is the soup: thick and smoky.

Ham hocks vary in saltiness. If using an unsmoked hock, the soup needs more seasoning at the end. A smoked hock gives the soup a deeper, more complex flavour and rarely needs additional salt. Taste before seasoning; oversalted split pea soup is hard to rescue.

items.name: Ham hock
items.prepNote: about 800 g, 1 kg, smoked or unsmoked

items.name: Water
items.prepNote: or enough to cover the hock

content.text: Rinse {{split-peas-yellow}} of split peas and put in the slow cooker pot with the {{onion}} sliced onions, {{carrot}} chopped carrots and {{celery}} chopped celery. Nestle the {{ham-hock}} ham hock on top. Tuck in {{bay-leaves}} of bay leaves and thyme sprigs. Pour over {{water}} of cold water; the hock should be mostly submerged. Add {{black-pepper}} of black pepper.

Fit the lid and cook on LOW for 8 to 9 hours or HIGH for 5 to 6 hours. The soup is ready when the peas have completely dissolved into the liquid and the ham falls easily from the bone when prodded with a spoon.

Lift the hock out onto a board. Pull the meat away from the bone with two forks, discarding skin, fat and bone. Return the shredded ham to the pot. Remove the bay leaves and thyme. Stir, taste for seasoning, and add salt only if needed. The soup should be thick and almost porridge-like; thin with a splash of boiling water if it's too thick to ladle.

items.fix: Cook for another hour on HIGH. Old dried peas sometimes need longer. Ensure the peas were not pre-soaked (pre-soaked peas can under-dissolve in a slow cooker).
items.cause: Temperature too low, or very old peas.

items.fix: Add a peeled raw potato to the pot and cook on HIGH for 30 minutes; it absorbs some salt. Do not add salt before tasting.
items.cause: Smoked or cured hock released a lot of salt.

items.fix: Stir in boiling water a ladleful at a time until it pours easily. The texture rehydrates with reheating.
items.cause: Normal for split peas; they gel as they cool.

content.text: Pea and ham soup is a winter staple in British households, associated with using up a gammon joint after Christmas or Easter. The slow cooker version makes it a no-effort Tuesday lunch: put it on before work, come home to a pot of thick, smoky soup. Serve in deep bowls with crusty bread and butter.
